# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes deployment YAML for our application stack. Include deployments for web application, Memcached cache, and background workers. Define services to expose Memcached for inter-pod communication. This is for our production Kubernetes cluster.
# Category: memcached_exposed_port

apiVersion: apps/v1 # Version of API group where object lives (apps in k8s) – version v1 from the first stable release, can be any older but not recommended to use directly on latest versions as we will have objects starting with a 'k' below it. We are using "app" because these types do exist - and if you create an app of type apps/v2 then kubelet may report errors (as v1 is obsolete)
kind: Deployment # Type to be created – we want our resource in this case being a 'Deployments' object. For other resources, use the appropriate API groups like batch / cpu etc.. In yaml files these are often indicated by their type and name (e.g., "Service", for services of particular kind)
metadata: # Information about your YAML - must be first item in a resource block – this is where you provide details to Kubernetes like the version, namespace etc... You'll want each one unique but not too different from others due so we use names. Names are usually derived by appending 's', e.g., DeploymentName
name: web-app # Name of your resource - this will be a prefix and suffix joined with '-'. The final name created on Kubernetes is ResourceType + “/” +  SpecifiedResourceKind (web application, memcached etc.) then we append Namespace in case there's one.
spec: # Information about how to create our object - this will be at the end of each resource block and usually described with comments if you want more detail on what kind / type it is – again joined by a hyphen for clarity, e.g., "This Deployment runs your Web Application ..." (with specifics)
   replicas: X # number of pod copies to be run at any given time - this should match the actual count in Pod Statuses and ReplicaSets but not required if it's set statically – must also include a selector field that matches labels onPod created by Deployment. This is important because once Kubernetes knows how many instances of your application to maintain, when new pod creation happens or old ones get terminated the count will change and then you’ll need an appropriate Update strategy
   template: # Describes what kind (container) should be made - this might also include ports if required. All things in templates are container-specific by default – containers use a Pod specification to set up themselves, not everything is just defined here as much like pods can have different mount points and environment variables ect...
     metadata: # Additional information about the Deployment instance - this will be at beginning of each Container's template section. In addition it has more fields such an IP address (if required), labels etc.. – we may add namespaces if needed, or others depending on your needs and requirements for Kubernetes
       name: web-app # Name set in the pod metadata will be a prefix join with '-' - e.g., 'webAppPod'. All instances of this Deployment instance are controlled by an Update strategy that allows rolling update – if one is not specified it defaults to Rolling updates (not all containers can participate at once and must wait until current ones finish)
     spec: # The specification for the container run inside our pods - there will be more here in case you have multiple components of your app running. But this could also contain things like how long each one should live, volume mount points etc... – just needs to match with actual application requirements (e.g., image name and ports)
       containers: # This field is a set for the container(s), which run inside our pod - you can add more than once if your app has multiple components ect… For each Container there’ll be required fields such as – Name, Image that contains Dockerfile info (Docker image to use and its version)
         name: web-app # Same prefix join with '-' in beginning of all containers. We add count so we can differentiate by namespace if necessary - e.g., 'webApp1', but must not include namespaces, as they would clash or cause conflict issues on Kubernetes side – only use alphanumeric characters and "-" for best results
         image: your-image # Docker Hub name / ID where to fetch the app’s code e.g., 'nginx' - we will also be including version if required in a real scenario due not all images might require specific versions (e, g docker pull nginx@version) – can include hash for private repositories as well
         ports: # Required when your container has multiple containers e.g., web and api that are different on Ports - it should be an array with required port numbers in each one being number mapped to a specific Port (can use named services too). In K8s pod we can map the network protocol as well like TCP, UDP etc...
           containerPort: X # Required when your Container has multiple containers e.g., web and api that are different on Ports - it should be an array with required port numbers in each one being number mapped to a specific Port (can use named services too). In K8s pod we can map the network protocol as well like TCP, UDP etc...
             containerPort: X  # Same prefix join together for clarity. Should match exact ports from web app configuration or used by application itself in case of running on different port e.g., '3012', but must be unique to avoid conflict with already declared Ports (like using an internal network). Note that we won’t declare it unless the container needs this particular specific Port mapping – otherwise you will have conflicts or miss some exposed ports
         imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent # Docker way of pulling and pushing images when they aren't present in K8s node already. For older versions might be necessary to keep things more simple by not mentioning it (it’ll just default as ‘Always') - but if we use one, then always try pull the image from registry
       restartPolicy: Always # The rule for restarts when a pod exits e.g., If killed or crashed due network issues etc… This will be set to 'Always' unless specified otherwise in K8s resource block of each container - this is also known as “Durability” at the same time, by default it’ll run even if there are problems with pod and other dependencies.
    selector: # How Pods should select which Deployment instance to control e..g., match labels for running in Kubernetes cluster (can include namespaces too). In case we have multiple instances of our web app on different port, or having them controlled by the same Service then they would all be required as mentioned here.
